text: robotic subtotal colectomy for synchronous colon cancers, and pan colitis. This case involves a twenty-five-year-old woman who presented with a history of three months of worsening lower abdominal pain, both in the right and left lower abdomen, thirty pounds of weight loss, anorexia, episodes of watery diarrhea, and intermittent bouts of emesis. There was no other significant medical or surgical history. Prior workup at an outside hospital included right upper quadrant ultrasound, HIDA scan, plain abdominal x-rays, and upper endoscopy, all of which were negative. Her symptoms persisted and she developed anemia with a hemoglobin of eight point eight. This prompted a CAT scan of the abdomen and pelvis. Imaging revealed diffuse wall thickening of the terminal ilium and cecum, edema of the right and transverse colon, findings suggestive of a mass in the right colon, and mesenteric adenopathy in the right lower quadrant with lymph nodes as large as one point four centimeters. Colonoscopy was performed, confirming a friable mass in the cecum, causing near obstruction, as well as severe colitis of the right colon through the distal transverse colon. Biopsies revealed moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma in the right colon mass, as well as high-grade dysplasia in the distal right colon, separate from the mass, a sessile serrated adenoma in the distal right colon, low-grade dysplasia in the transverse colon, and normal mucosa in the left sigmoid and rectum. A CEA level at this time was one point eight. Because of the right colon cancer, the documentation of high-grade dysplasia and low-grade dysplasia in the right and transverse colon, and the patient's age, robotic-assisted subtotal colectomy was recommended by consensus of a multi-specialty tumor committee.

The da Vinci SI robotic system is used with two dockings for the procedure. The camera is positioned in the inframammary area, with ports placed in the left upper quadrant, right lower quadrant, and left lower quadrant. Laparoscopic exploration reveals local invasion of the right colon mass into the abdominal wall, with no signs of carcinomatosis or metastatic disease. The patient is rotated left side down, and the robotic cart is docked over the right upper abdomen.

The surgeon begins lateral dissection using a monopolar scissor to remove the abdominal wall with the specimen, aiming for a negative radial margin. The monopolar scissor is used for sharp dissection, while the fenestrated bipolar grasper aids in blunt retraction. After lateral mobilization, the ileal mesentery is scored, and the underwrist vessel sealer efficiently harvests the ileal vessels to the cecum. The right colic pedicle is elevated with the fenestrated bipolar grasper, and a plane is developed between retroperitoneal and peritoneal structures using the monopolar scissor. The ureter is identified and protected. Dissection continues up the right gutter along the white line of Toldt towards the hepatic flexure. Fine dissection with the monopolar scissors allows for skeletonization and high ligation of the right colic pedicle with the vessel sealer. Avascular attachments of the mesocolon and duodenum are identified.

The surgeon separates the omentum from the transverse colon along embryologic planes, opening the lesser sac. Enlarged lymph nodes in the transverse mesocolon are visible with magnification. The monopolar scissors and fenestrated bipolar are used for precise dissection along avascular planes, separating the transverse colon and mesocolon from the duodenum and pancreas, and enabling high ligation of the middle colic pedicle. The transverse colon is further separated from the omentum distally. The greater curvature of the stomach, transverse colon, and small intestine are identified to orient the dissection planes.

The robotic system is repositioned for a second docking, with an eight millimeter port placed in the right upper quadrant and adjustments made to the assistant ports. The patient is rotated right side down, and the patient cart is docked over the left upper quadrant. The surgeon dissects the left colon off the retroperitoneum in a lateral to medial fashion, noting significant tissue edema due to partial obstruction and colitis, which aids in defining the dissection planes. The left colic pedicle is identified and transected using the endorhist vessel sealer.

After completing the mobilization of the splenic flexure and left colon, the robotic instruments are removed, and the patient cart is undocked. The surgeon performs the resection through a fan and steel incision, eviscerating the entire colon and creating a side-to-side anastomosis between the terminal ileum and sigmoid colon.

The patient's post-operative course was uneventful. An enhanced recovery protocol included preoperative tap block with liposomal bupivacaine, early ambulation, oral intake starting on postoperative day zero, IV acetaminophen, PO-alvimepan, and minimization of narcotic use. The patient was discharged home on postoperative day four. Final pathology reviewed a moderate to poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma in the cecum, T four and zero, and a well to moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma in the right colon, T three and zero. Fifty-six negative lymph nodes were in the specimen. All margins were negative. This included a three centimeter circumferential margin at the abdominal wall. Multifocal low grade dysplasia was noted in the remainder of the specimen. No adjuvant therapy was recommended.
At six months follow up, the patient was doing well with no evidence of disease.
